tracker_summary.inventor: Spencer Silver (adhesive, 1968); Art Fry (application concept, 1974); 3M Company (commercialization)
tracker_summary.current_use: Over 50 billion sold annually in 100+ countries; one of 3M's most profitable product lines
tracker_summary.initial_funding_reason: Developing an ultra-strong structural adhesive for aerospace applications

the_x: 3M's aerospace adhesive research program trying to create a stronger bonding agent
the_y: The Post-it Note — one of the most commercially successful office products in history, born from an adhesive rejected for being too weak
summary: 3M chemist Spencer Silver set out in 1968 to create a super-strong aerospace adhesive. He created the opposite: microspheres that stuck lightly and could be repositioned without residue. Silver spent years promoting his 'solution without a problem' internally. Six years later, colleague Art Fry sat in church frustrated that his hymnal bookmark kept falling out. He remembered Silver's adhesive. Even then, 3M's marketing resisted for years before a 'Boise Blitz' test market — giving away free samples — achieved a 90% repurchase rate and launched a national product.

chain.where: 3M Central Research Laboratory, Maplewood (St. Paul), Minnesota
chain.what_they_were_doing: Attempting to synthesize a high-strength structural adhesive for aerospace by experimenting with acrylate copolymer microsphere formulations. The goal was maximum bond strength.
chain.the_jump: Silver accidentally created an adhesive with the opposite properties: tiny acrylic spheres forming a low-tack, pressure-sensitive bond that peeled off cleanly and could be reapplied without losing effectiveness. It stuck but didn't bond.
chain.funding: 3M Company internal R&D (Central Research Laboratory budget).

chain.what_they_were_doing: Evangelizing his adhesive internally through seminars and lobbying product managers. Silver called it a 'solution looking for a problem.' No one could find a use case.
chain.funding: 3M internal. Silver's persistent promotion was only possible because of 3M's culture of tolerating exploratory research.

chain.what_they_were_doing: Repeatedly evaluating and declining Silver's adhesive. A bulletin board coated with it was trialed internally but generated no commercial interest. The adhesive sat unused.

chain.who: Arthur 'Art' Fry (3M product development engineer and choir member)
chain.where: North Presbyterian Church, North St. Paul, Minnesota (concept); 3M labs (development)
chain.what_they_were_doing: Singing in church choir, frustrated that paper bookmark slips kept falling out of his hymnal. During a Sunday service, recalled attending one of Silver's internal seminars.
chain.the_jump: Fry realized a bookmark coated with Silver's adhesive would stay in place but remove cleanly. He prototyped the first adhesive bookmark using 3M's '15% rule' for self-directed projects. The concept evolved from bookmark to general-purpose note.
chain.funding: 3M Company (Fry used company time under the 15% discretionary research policy).

chain.what_they_were_doing: Solving manufacturing challenges. Fry reportedly built a coating machine in his home basement. Distributed samples to 3M executives' secretaries — who kept coming back for more. First evidence of organic demand, yet internal marketing studies still predicted no market.
chain.funding: 3M internal. Geoff Nicholson and Joe Ramey provided crucial internal sponsorship.

chain.where: Four US test cities; then Boise, Idaho
chain.what_they_were_doing: Launched as 'Press 'n Peel' in Richmond, Tampa, Tulsa, Denver — results poor because consumers couldn't understand without trying. Then the 'Boise Blitz': flooding Boise with free samples achieved a 90% repurchase rate.
chain.the_jump: The Boise test proved consumer sampling — not advertising — was the key. The product needed to be experienced, not described. Four failed test markets almost killed it.

chain.where: National US launch April 6, 1980; international rollout followed
chain.what_they_were_doing: Renamed from 'Press 'n Peel' to 'Post-it' after consumer feedback. Within a year, 3M's best-selling new product. By 1984, sold in 50 countries. Never required significant reformulation.
chain.the_jump: The 12-year journey from Silver's accidental discovery to national launch — through years of internal rejection, a church choir eureka moment, four failed test markets, and one decisive free-sample blitz — is now a canonical innovation case study.

sources: Nayak PR, Ketteringham JM. 'Breakthroughs!' Rawson Associates. 1986. (Primary-source interviews with Silver and Fry)
3M Company. 'The History of the Post-it Note.' https://www.3m.com/3M/en_US/post-it-us/contact-us/about-us/
Silver SM. U.S. Patent 3,691,140. 'Acrylate Copolymer Microspheres.' 1972. https://patents.google.com/patent/US3691140
Fry A. 'The Post-it Note: An Intrapreneurial Success.' SAM Advanced Management Journal. 1987;52(3):4-9.
Petroski H. 'The Post-it Note: Stuck on Success.' American Scientist. 2010. https://www.americanscientist.org/article/the-post-it-note
